content.full_text:
VIA FAX AND SURFACE MAIL

March 20, 1992

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

POSSIBLE CONFLICT OF INTEREST OF MOUNTAIN VIEW CITY
COUNCILMEMBER DENA BONNELL

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

With this letter, I request a written advice letter on a conflict-of-interest question
with regard to City of Mountain View Councilmember Dena Bonnell.

City Councilmember Dena Bonnell is the Controller of Valley Oil Company. By
virtue of her employment she derives an income in excess of $250 per year.

Valley Oil Company is a small, closely held corporation, not traded and not qualified
to trade on any market or exchange. The company does not meet the standards for
listing on any exchange or market. Councilmember Bonnell is not a stockholder
and has no investment or equity interest in Valley Oil Company. Her
compensation is entirely salary or wages derived from her employment. Her
compensation is not tied in any way to the amount of gross revenues or net profit of
her employer.

Valley Oil Co.'s total gross revenues are approximately $28.million if tax collections
are excluded; if tax collections are included, gross receipts are approximately $40
million. For determination of material effect on a source of income, Valley Oil
Company appears to fall under the materiality provisions set out in Regulation (2
CCR) 18702.2(g).

In her ordinary duties as the Controller for Valley Oil Company, Councilmember
Bonnell does not handle customer lists or deal with customer accounts on a name
basis. She does not account for receipts, the quantities of oil products those receipts
represent, and does not compute or determine tax payments due to the State and
Federal agencies which collect tax on oil products. With one minor exception, the

Recycled Paper

Scott Hallabrin
March 20, 1992
Page 2

various taxes are not reported in the name of the customer. Councilmember
Bonnell does prepare and file applications for refunds of taxes remitted when
accounts are written off as uncollectable or as bad debts. In such case, she would
become familiar with the identity of a customer of Valley Oil involved in any
particular transaction. Valley Oil Co.'s business records are organized such that all
of the financial records reflect entries without taxes, except for those records which
relate directly to the reporting of those taxes to the collecting agencies.

Councilmember Bonnell became aware of Mountain View Golf, Inc.'s relationship
to Valley Oil Co. in a casual conversation with other employees of Valley Oil Co.

Mountain View Golf, Inc.'s major business is to lease and operate the Shoreline
Golf Links, a municipal golf course owned by the City of Mountain View.
Mountain View Golf currently is operating under a 25-year lease on the Shoreline
Golf Links, which will expire in the year 2013. Mountain View Golf has requested a
renegotiation of the Shoreline Golf Links lease with the City of Mountain View.

In the course of discussions regarding the renegotiation, Mountain View Golf has
sought relief from repayment of all or a portion of more than $2 million in debt
associated with purchase of the leasehold, which was originally held by another
operator. Mountain View Golf has proposed, in exchange for the relief from such
debt to invest a substantial sum (more than $1 million) of capital investment in
improvements in the golf course.

Mountain View Golf may be in some financial difficulty. Some payments under the
lease have been made late, and annual audits required by the lease for the benefit of
the City indicate that the company may be in financial distress. It is, therefore,
important for Mountain View Golf to attempt a successful renegotiation of the Golf
Course lease. From the point of view of the City of Mountain View, the City is
concerned about some significant potential breaches of the existing lease and must
consider whether to initiate action to address those issues of breach.

Mountain View Golf purchases oil products in the course of its operation of the
Shoreline Golf Links under the lease. Mountain View Golf purchases approximately $1,081 worth of oil products per month, or approximately $12,972 worth per
year. The business derived by Valley Oil Company from Mountain View Golf
Company represents a very small proportion of the total gross revenue of Valley Oil
Company, whether measured with or without the tax collections--less than .03%
(0.0003) of all receipts.

Approximately 30 percent of the total annual amount payable from Mountain View
Golf to Valley Oil Company ($3,891) is derived solely from Federal and State taxes

Scott Hallabrin
March 20, 1992
Page 3

which Valley Oil Company is required by law to collect and thereupon remit to the
State and Federal agencies responsible for collection of the tax. Valley Oil
Company's function with relation to the tax is to collect and remit the taxes at the
same time that Valley Oil Company collects for oil products which are delivered.
Typically, Valley Oil Company remits the tax based on invoices showing deliveries
to its customers. If a particular invoice results in a bad debt that is eventually
written off, Valley Oil Company can seek and obtain a refund for the tax paid on
which are delivered. If the payment of taxes is deducted, the remaining receipts
available to Valley Oil Company is approximately $800 per month, or $9,600 per
year.

Other pertinent information is that Mountain View Golf Company currently has an
unpaid account receivable of approximately $7,700. For purposes of this letter, it
should be assumed that Mountain View Golf may be untimely as to payment of all
or a portion of this account receivable.

It is clear that if the gross revenues derived by Valley Oil Company from Mountain
View Golf Company exceeds $10,000 per year, Councilmember Bonnell is disqualified from participating in actions involving Mountain View Golf Company under
Regulation 18702.2(g)(1). The question is whether or not the tax collections for
which Valley Oil Company is a conduit must be included in the determination of
gross revenues derived from Mountain View Golf Company. While a literal interpretation of the language of the rule would seem to indicate that the tax payments
are included in the term "gross revenues," Valley Oil Company derives no benefit
from these payments, is legally obligated to remit them to the government within
10 to 30 days of receipt, and derives no practical use or benefit from the funds. In
fact, it is a significant business inconvenience for Valley Oil Company to collect the
taxes and remit them to the government because of the necessity and responsibility
for filing accompanying returns and the risk that handling the funds entails. Valley
Oil Company is in the oil business, not the tax collecting business.

Councilmember Bonnell contends, and I agree, that despite the literal words of the
above-mentioned regulation, the amount of taxes collected on behalf of oil products
purchasers like Mountain View Golf should not be included in any determination
of gross revenues for determining thresholds under the Fair Political Practices Act.
In other contexts, income received from a governmental entity is not considered
income which disqualifies a public official from participation in a decision, and in
this case, the tax collections are entirely for the convenience and benefit of the
government entities which are entitled to collect tax and provide no benefit to
Councilmember Bonnell's employer. Under these circumstances, it appears that it
is not reasonably foreseeable that Councilmember Bonnell's participation in this

Scott Hallabrin
March 20, 1992
Page 4

decision would have a material effect on her source of income from Valley Oil
Company as is contemplated by the Regulations or the Fair Political Practices Act.

We request the Commission staff to provide an advice letter indicating: (1) whether
or not tax collections of the type described above are includable in gross revenues for
purposes of interpreting Regulation 18702.2(g); and (2) whether or not, under the
above-described circumstances, it can be said that Councilmember Bonnell has a
conflict of interest as described under the Act.

It is critical that we receive a response to this request as soon as possible. A decision
on this matter must be made shortly, and the Council has continued the matter for
the purposes of resolving the conflict issue until March 25 or March 26, 1992.
Councilmember Bonnell believes that the decision is extremely important to the
City and that her participation in the decision is necessary for the best interests of the
City.

We would appreciate a response prior to the close of business on March 25, 1992, if
that is at all possible. If a written advice letter cannot be provided, a telephone call
from the appropriate staff member advising of your conclusions would be appreciated prior to that time.

Certain aspects of this question were discussed briefly by telephone conversation
between Bianca Breeze of your office and Myra Prestidge, an Assistant City Attorney
in my office. Ms. Breeze indicated that she would perform some preliminary
research on the definition of "gross revenues," and it may be appropriate to include
her information in your review of this matter.

I am available to answer any questions or provide any further information which
you may need to respond to this question. I can be reached during normal business
hours at (415) 903-6303 or before 8 a.m. or after 5 p.m. at (415) 903-6398. Either of
these numbers will provide voice mail 24 hours a day if there is no answer. Please
reply to the Fax number indicated on the letterhead.

Thank you for your consideration.

Very truly yours,

P. Lawrence Klose
City Attorney

cc: Councilmember Bonnell, City Council, CM

sections.question: The question is whether or not the tax collections for which Valley Oil Company is a conduit must be included in the determination of gross revenues derived from Mountain View Golf Company. While a literal interpretation of the language of the rule would seem to indicate that the tax payments are included in the term 'gross revenues,' Valley Oil Company derives no benefit from these payments, is legally obligated to remit them to the government within 10 to 30 days of receipt, and derives no practical use or benefit from the funds. We request the Commission staff to provide an advice letter indicating: (1) whether or not tax collections of the type described above are includable in gross revenues for purposes of interpreting Regulation 18702.2(g); and (2) whether or not, under the above-described circumstances, it can be said that Councilmember Bonnell has a conflict of interest as described under the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether tax collections that Valley Oil Company remits on behalf of a customer (Mountain View Golf, Inc.) should be included in calculating gross revenues under Regulation 18702.2(g) to determine if City Councilmember Dena Bonnell has a material financial conflict of interest. The underlying issue is whether Councilmember Bonnell, employed as Controller at Valley Oil, can participate in City decisions regarding a golf course lease negotiation with Mountain View Golf, Inc., which purchases approximately $72,972 annually in oil products from Valley Oil.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from City Attorney to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No CONCLUSION section present. Document contains detailed QUESTION section explaining the circumstances and requesting specific guidance on two points. Requestor seeks urgent response before March 25, 1992. Document ID 92178 from 1992.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The question is whether or not the tax collections for which Valley Oil Company is a conduit must be included in the determination of gross revenues derived from Mountain View Golf Company. While a literal interpretation of the language of the rule would seem to indicate that the tax payments are included in the term 'gross revenues,' Valley Oil Company derives no benefit from these payments, is legally obligated to remit them to the government within 10 to 30 days of receipt, and derives no practical use or benefit from the funds. We request the Commission staff to provide an advice letter indicating: (1) whether or not tax collections of the type described above are includable in gross revenues for purposes of interpreting Regulation 18702.2(g); and (2) whether or not, under the above-described circumstances, it can be said that Councilmember Bonnell has a conflict of interest as described under the Act.

embedding.summary: City of Mountain View requests an FPPC advice letter regarding whether Councilmember Dena Bonnell has a conflict of interest in participating in lease negotiations with Mountain View Golf, Inc., which is a customer of her employer Valley Oil Company. The key issue is whether government tax collections that Valley Oil merely facilitates should be included in calculating gross revenues to determine conflict of interest thresholds.
